tossups.question: The life of a fictional person from this location is described in a preface by Pierre Louÿs (“loo-EESE”) that discusses how crime was inspired by its tyrant Pittacus enacting an anti-flute law. Two monodic poets from this location who are part of the nine Melic poets each lend their name to a four-line stanza form. Both Alcaeus and a contemporary who described feeling “greener than grass” in a poem opening “He seems to me equal to the gods” hailed from the city of Mytilene on this island. A poetess from this island pleads with a “deathless” “child of Zeus” to “be my ally” in a translation of her “Hymn to Aphrodite” collected in Anne Carson’s If Not, Winter. For 10 points, Sappho is from what island, whose name is the origin of the term for female same-sex love?

Both Alcaeus and a contemporary who described feeling "greener than grass" in a poem opening "He seems to me equal to the gods" hailed from the city of Mytilene on this island. A poetess from this island pleads with a "deathless" "child of Zeus" to "be my ally" in a translation of her "Hymn to Aphrodite" collected in Anne Carson's If Not, Winter. tossups.question: This figure is the subject of the best known play by Estelle Anna Lewis, whom Edgar Allan Poe referred to as this figure’s “rival”. Cupid rebels against Venus and lives with this figure after falling in love with her in an early comedy by John Lyly (“lily”). A “play in verse” about this figure is the second play by Lawrence Durrell. Durrell also named his second daughter, with whom he may have had an incestuous relationship, after this figure. This figure believes that the slave Melitta has seduced the youth this figure loves, leading this figure to commit suicide, in the best known tragedy by (*) Franz Grillparzer. Nearly a dozen attested lost plays by Ancient Greek comic playwrights center on this woman, some of which are titled for the Leucadian cliffs, which this woman supposedly jumped off of to kill herself due to her unrequited love for the ferryman Phaon. For 10 points, name this common subject of biographical speculation, a poet from the island of Lesbos.
tossups.question_sanitized: This figure is the subject of the best known play by Estelle Anna Lewis, whom Edgar Allan Poe referred to as this figure's "rival". This figure believes that the slave Melitta has seduced the youth this figure loves, leading this figure to commit suicide, in the best known tragedy by (*) Franz Grillparzer. Nearly a dozen attested lost plays by Ancient Greek comic playwrights center on this woman, some of which are titled for the Leucadian cliffs, which this woman supposedly jumped off of to kill herself due to her unrequited love for the ferryman Phaon. For 10 points, name this common subject of biographical speculation, a poet from the island of Lesbos.
tossups.answer: Sappho of Lesbos (The Lyly play is Sapho and Phao.)

tossups.question: After leaving the Academy, Aristotle moved to this island, where he married Pythias and researched botany with one of his students. Strabo credits the invention of the seven-string lyre to a poet from this island. The dactylic and "skolion," an early form of drinking song, were introduced by a poet from island named Terpander, who was from the city Antissa. Cleon and Diotus debated whether to slaughter the inhabitants of this island after a revolt during the Peloponnesian War that occurred in this island's city of Mytilene. A poet from this island supposedly committed suicide by jumping from the Leucadian cliffs and was a contemporary of the poet Alceus. For 10 points, name this island home to the "tenth muse," the Greek poet Sappho.

tossups.question: This collection includes poems beginning “for you beautiful ones my thought / is not changeable” and “their heart grew cold / they let their wings down”. The speaker of another poem in this collection says, “I beg you / do not break with hard pains” and describes “what I want to happen most of all / in my crazy (+) heart”. A poem in this collection describes a man who “opposite you / sits and listens close / to your sweet speaking”. (*) This collection includes poems titled for a “Deathless” figure of “the spangled mind” and one often titled phainetai moi. A line from Fragment 22 titles, for 10 points, what collection of Sappho translations by Anne Carson?

tossups.question: One of this author’s works expresses a wish that one of the central figures will “raise up his head and become a man,” and that the other will return with his ship undamaged. Another work by this author opens with a description of its addressee’s “many-colored throne” and chariot drawn by sparrows. A (*) metrical form named for this poet is notable for its “Adonic line,” which consists of a dactyl and a spondee. Anne Carson’s If Not, Winter is a set of adaptations of works by this poet. In a work by this native of Mytilene, the speaker is “paler than grass,” in contrast to an individual who seems “equal to the gods” due to his ability to keep his composure in the presence of the addressee. That poem was later adapted as Catullus’s Poem 51. This poet wrote in the Aeolic dialect, and other than the aforementioned Hymn to Aphrodite, most of her works only exist in fragmentary form. For 10 points, name this ancient Greek poet from Lesbos.

tossups.question: Marilyn Hacker wrote a poem about how this author’s “guts clutched up like this.” This author wrote that “a human being without old age is not a possibility” in a poem translated under the title “The Beat Goes On.” In a poem by this author, the speaker says “it is as if my tongue has broken and immediately a subtle fire has run over my skin” while watching a man sitting opposite a woman and listening to her laughing. tossups.question: This poet suggests we should place all our trust in the gods, “since fair winds swiftly follow harsh gales”. This poet is recorded as going into exile in Sicily on the Marmor Parium. The first possible reception of this poet is a fragment of Anacreon which laments that his hair is now white. A later comedy is probably the source of their supposed spouse, “Little Prick from the Isle of Man”. tossups.question: In 2014, a team led by Dirk Obbink found this author’s “Brothers Poem” in a private collection. A poem by this author is only preserved in the treatise On the Sublime, which praises its intensity, and opens by calling a man a peer of the gods. bonuses.leadin: A fragmentary poem praises this poet as “you with strands of hair in violet, O holy one, you with the honey-sweet smile.” For 10 points each:

parts: Name this poet from Lesbos, who wrote the “Hymn to Aphrodite.”

This contemporary of Sappho praised her “honey-sweet smile.” He wrote that “We should not surrender our hearts to our troubles, / for we shall make no headway by grieving” in a stanza form named for him.
In an Alcaeus fragment, the speaker calls for this action without “waiting for the lamps.” A Horace ode about Cleopatra begins “now is the time for [this action].”

bonuses.leadin: Answer some questions about Renée Vivien, a British poet prominent in the literary circles of Belle Époque Paris, for 10 points each.
parts: In 1903, Vivien published a translation of this poet, reflecting Vivien's openly lesbian identity. Her translation includes this ancient Greek poet's "Ode to Aphrodite."
This author was Vivien's neighbor in Paris, and depicted Vivien in The Pure and the Impure. This author, usually known by a single name, portrayed a young Parisian woman in the novella Gigi.

This author's poetry collection The Alms of Alcippe (al-KIP-ee) won the annual Renée Vivien Prize in 1958. This author, the lifelong partner of Grace Frick, wrote a novel whose title ruler describes the death of his lover Antinous (an-TI-noh-us).
answers: Marguerite Yourcenar [or Marguerite de Crayencour, or Marguerite Antoinette Jeanne Marie Ghislaine Cleenewerck de Crayencour] (The novel is Memoirs of Hadrian.)

bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A book by Anne Carson claims that it was this person who first called eros "bittersweet" and that "no one who has been in love disputes her." For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this poet whose works were translated in If Not, Winter, including a poem addressed to a "deathless" goddess that begs "do not break with hard pains... my heart."

"Ode to Aphrodite" is Sappho's only surviving poem with this quality. Brackets are used in If Not, Winter to indicate that Sappho's other poems are fragmentary, thus [emphasize] lacking this quality. A description is acceptable.
A moment when Delphis enters Simaetha's house in this ancient poet's Second Idyll was adapted from Sappho 31. This poet is considered the inventor of bucolic poetry.

bonuses.leadin: This poem describes how “tongue breaks and thin / fire is racing under skin / and in eyes no sight and drumming / fills ears.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poem that begins by describing a man who “seems to me equal to gods.” This poem abruptly ends mid-sentence with the line “But all is to be dared, because even a person of poverty.” You may also give the opening words in Greek by which this poem is alternately known.
Fragment 31 is a poem by this ancient Greek woman poet from the island of Lesbos.
This first Fragment by Sappho is also one of her only complete surviving poems. It addresses a “deathless” goddess “of the spangled mind” who “twists lures.”
answers: Fragment 31 [accept phainetai moi; accept, but DO NOT REVEAL, Sappho 31 or answers that mention a poem or Fragment by Sappho and 31; prompt on “Jealousy”]
answers_sanitized: Fragment 31 [accept phainetai moi; accept, but DO NOT REVEAL, Sappho 31 or answers that mention a poem or Fragment by Sappho and 31; prompt on "Jealousy"]

Fragment 31 is a poem by this ancient Greek woman poet from the island of Lesbos.
This first Fragment by Sappho is also one of her only complete surviving poems. It addresses a "deathless" goddess "of the spangled mind" who "twists lures."

bonuses.leadin: A poem often ascribed to this author is set while the narrator “sleeps alone” at midnight, when “the moon and the Pleiades have set” and “the time is passing.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this female Greek poet from Lesbos who wrote of an “iridescent-throned” goddess descending in a chariot pulled by sparrows in the “Hymn to Aphrodite.”

A recurring Greek literary tale in which Sappho jumps off a rock into the sea after being rejected by Phaon first appeared in a play by this author. This comedic playwright wrote of the grouchy old man Knemon in Dyskolos.

“The Last Song of Sappho” is a poem from this author’s Canti. This Italian poet’s reflections on the relative merits of Latin and modern Romance languages occupy many pages of his massive set of notes, the Zibaldone (“dzee-ball-DOH-nay”).

"The Last Song of Sappho" is a poem from this author's Canti. This Italian poet's reflections on the relative merits of Latin and modern Romance languages occupy many pages of his massive set of notes, the Zibaldone ("dzee-ball-DOH-nay").

bonuses.leadin: Aside from Homer, Sappho is probably the most famous person to write hymns to Aphrodite. Answer some questions about her. For 10 points each:
parts: In addition to being known as "The Poetess," Sappho was also given this title, indicating that she was an additional member of a group of artistic goddesses. Anne Bradstreet wrote a collection titled for a figure of this title having "Lately Sprung Up In America."
This book by Anne Carson is a collection and translation of all of Sappho's extant works and fragments, presented as they exist on the fragmented papyri.
answers_sanitized: If Not, Winter: Fragments of Sappho
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Aside from Homer, Sappho is probably the most famous person to write hymns to Aphrodite. Answer some questions about her. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: In addition to being known as "The Poetess," Sappho was also given this title, indicating that she was an additional member of a group of artistic goddesses. Anne Bradstreet wrote a collection titled for a figure of this title having "Lately Sprung Up In America."
This book by Anne Carson is a collection and translation of all of Sappho's extant works and fragments, presented as they exist on the fragmented papyri.

bonuses.leadin: An elegiac couplet typically consists of a line in this meter followed by a line in pentameter. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poetic meter often described as “epic” for its use in such works as the Iliad, Odyssey, and Aeneid.
Theseus’s abandonment of Ariadne is depicted in a long ekphrastic passage of this epyllion in dactylic hexameter that centers on the wedding of Peleus and Thetis. It is the longest poem by Catullus.
Catullus 51 mostly translates a fragment in which this poet described a lover who seems “to be equal to a god.” Both poems use the stanza named for this female poet from Lesbos.
answers: dactylic hexameter [prompt on hexameter; prompt on epic meter or heroic hexameter]
answers_sanitized: dactylic hexameter [prompt on hexameter; prompt on epic meter or heroic hexameter]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: An elegiac couplet typically consists of a line in this meter followed by a line in pentameter. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this poetic meter often described as "epic" for its use in such works as the Iliad, Odyssey, and Aeneid.
Theseus's abandonment of Ariadne is depicted in a long ekphrastic passage of this epyllion in dactylic hexameter that centers on the wedding of Peleus and Thetis. It is the longest poem by Catullus.
Catullus 51 mostly translates a fragment in which this poet described a lover who seems "to be equal to a god." Both poems use the stanza named for this female poet from Lesbos.

bonuses.leadin: This poet included poems such as “Amazon” and “Words to My Love” in her 1909 collection A Crown of Violets. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this British-born French lesbian poet who was heavily influenced by Parnassianism and Symbolism. She carried on an affair with the author Natalie Clifford Barney, who would go on to win her namesake literary prize in 1949.
Vivien is often compared to this ancient Greek poet from Lesbos, who wrote of her desire for another woman in “Ode to Aphrodite.”
Vivien’s highly sexual and hedonistic lifestyle was the subject of The Pure and the Impure, a “pen-portrait” by this Frenchwoman and friend of Vivien who also wrote the novella Gigi.
answers: Renée Vivien [or Pauline Mary Tarn; accept le Prix Renée Vivien or the Renée Vivien Prize]
answers_sanitized: Renee Vivien [or Pauline Mary Tarn; accept le Prix Renee Vivien or the Renee Vivien Prize]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This poet included poems such as "Amazon" and "Words to My Love" in her 1909 collection A Crown of Violets. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this British-born French lesbian poet who was heavily influenced by Parnassianism and Symbolism. She carried on an affair with the author Natalie Clifford Barney, who would go on to win her namesake literary prize in 1949.
Vivien is often compared to this ancient Greek poet from Lesbos, who wrote of her desire for another woman in "Ode to Aphrodite."
Vivien's highly sexual and hedonistic lifestyle was the subject of The Pure and the Impure, a "pen-portrait" by this Frenchwoman and friend of Vivien who also wrote the novella Gigi.

bonuses.leadin: Anne Carson collected this writer’s poetry in the publication If Not, Winter. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this “Tenth Muse” who opened one poem by describing its title character as “iridescent-throned” and “deathless”.
Sappho’s most famous extant work is an ode to this Greek goddess of erotic love. The most common Homeric epithet for this goddess is “laughter-loving”.
A much later “Tenth Muse” is Sor Juana, a polymath who lived in this modern-day country. A later poet from this country penned the collections Luna Silvestre and Eagle or Sun.
